analysis:
Across the stronger heuristics I see the same effective pipeline: (1) deterministic Pareto prefiltering, (2) a cheap per-point proxy (axis-aligned box volume and/or few-weight scalarizations) to rank and build an O(k)-proportional shortlist, (3) a CELF-style lazy greedy using cached singleton HVs (with selective pairwise caches when the shortlist is small), and (4) a bounded 1-for-1 swap local search. Top variants differ only in shortlist sizing, weight-vector design (Dirichlet vs. simple random draws), and how aggressively they prune via cheap upper bounds or cap HV calls.

Weak/inefficient variants either (a) recompute full hypervolumes too often (nested loops, frequent vstack/pg.hypervolume calls), (b) lack caching or pairwise reuse, (c) use large uncontrolled HV budgets or naive randomized initialization (GSI_LS) that forces many expensive HV evaluations, or (d) build too-large shortlists that blow up exact HV cost. Common micro-inefficiencies: O(N^2) Python-level dominance loops (acceptable for moderate N but wasteful for larger N), repeated list membership checks, repeated allocation (stacking) when evaluating candidates, and inconsistent handling of edge-cases (k=0, N<k) in a few variants.

The best tradeoffs: (i) small deterministic shortlists focused on nondominated/high-proxy points, (ii) cached singleton HVs and selective pairwise HVs to validate lazy scores, (iii) CELF lazy timestamps to avoid recomputing marginal gains until necessary, and (iv) tight, reproducible swap passes with an HV-call budget. Approximate HVC via many weight vectors (Heuristic 10/11) can reduce exact HV calls but must be tuned (vector count, normalization) and paired with CELF to recover accuracy. Deterministic tie-breaking and seeded RNGs make results reproducible; explicit HV-call budgets and cheap upper bounds (box-volume vs. current_hv) control runtime while preserving most of the HV gain.
experience: For the next generation, combine: adaptive shortlist sizing (scale with pareto size and k), CELF lazy greedy with cached singletons + selective pairwise HVs, and tight upper-bound pruning (box-vol or scalar HVC) to avoid useless HV calls. Add an HV-call budget, incremental HV updates, and prioritize swap candidates via proxy gains or diversity. Vectorize dominance checks and weight computations; keep all randomness seeded and tie-break deterministically.
current_reflection: For the next generation, combine: adaptive shortlist sizing (scale with pareto size and k), CELF lazy greedy with cached singletons + selective pairwise HVs, and tight upper-bound pruning (box-vol or scalar HVC) to avoid useless HV calls. Add an HV-call budget, incremental HV updates, and prioritize swap candidates via proxy gains or diversity. Vectorize dominance checks and weight computations; keep all randomness seeded and tie-break deterministically.
good_reflections: Prefilter dominated points, approximate HVC with few weight vectors to shortlist candidates, then perform a local search using cached incremental HVC deltas. Minimize Python-side HV calls by batching and avoid nested HV recomputations. Make the algorithm deterministic (seeded) and ensure it always returns exactly k points.
bad_reflections: Favor adaptive shortlists driven by pareto size/diversity, use cheap box+scalar proxies to prune, then run CELF with cached singleton HVs and selective pairwise caching (only when shortlist ≤ threshold). Enforce an HV‑call budget, apply tight upper‑bound pruning, and use bounded deterministic 1‑for‑1 swaps. Tune weight counts and shortlist size adaptively (not fixed), exploit vectorization/parallelism, and prefer incremental or library‑level HV updates to minimize expensive recomputations.

Prefer small adaptive shortlists (O(k) with Pareto awareness), use CELF with robust singleton/pairwise caches and box‑vol upper bounds, cap HV calls, vectorize dominance/proxy computations, avoid Python-level list removals in hot loops, and evaluate swaps via marginal deltas or cheap pruning before full HV calls. Exploit low‑D exact HV routines when available and keep deterministic tie‑breaking.

Favor deterministic Pareto prefilter, vectorized box+scalar proxies to build an O(k) shortlist, precompute singleton HVs, run CELF lazy greedy with timestamps, conditionally cache pairs for small shortlists, apply tight cheap upper bounds to prune, cap HV calls, and use efficient numpy masks/sets. These deliver high-quality hypervolume with far fewer expensive HV evaluations.
comprehensive_reflection:
- Keywords: adaptive shortlist, CELF lazy greedy, cached singletons, selective pairwise cache, box-vol/scalar HVC, HV-call budget, incremental HV, vectorized proxies, deterministic seed, return-exact-k
- Advice: Prefilter dominated points; build an adaptive O(k)-scale shortlist (Pareto+diversity aware) with few weight vectors or box/scalar proxies; run CELF with cached singleton HVs, selectively cache pairs for small shortlists, prune via tight upper bounds, batch/increment HV updates, cap HV calls, vectorize hot computations, seed RNG and tie-break deterministically so the algorithm always returns exactly k.
- Avoid: heavy Python loops/list removals in hot paths, nested/redundant HV recomputations, unbounded HV evaluations, non-deterministic tie-breaking, blind pairwise caching on large shortlists.
- Explanation: Pruning + lazy evaluation + caching minimizes expensive HV calls, yields near‑optimal hypervolume faster, scales well and is reproducible.
